sections.content:
The term 'data' has always captured my interest, how to nicely visualize it, communicate it, understand it. Building my portfolio website I really attempted to work with this personal interest. Instead of relying on a CMS like WordPress, I built the site in Svelte as a fully data-driven system where projects are structured in JSON and can be searched or filtered by tags, tools, or programming languages. Designing the site this way made me realize how much the structure of data shapes how information can be explored. The portfolio stopped feeling like a collection of pages and more like a dataset that could be navigated from different perspectives.

The Data Physicalization Project revealed another dimension of working with data. Our team created a museum installation translating planetary datasets into physical interactions using 3D-printed planets, mechanical dials, and temperature output. Designing those mappings made me aware that data that is analytically correct does not automatically become experientially meaningful. What matters is how variables are translated into forms that people can intuitively interpret.

The M12 study required engaging with data from a research perspective. We conducted a mixed pre/post experiment with 41 participants to test whether feedback style affected novice programmers' confidence. Running the analysis required actually understanding what a mixed-design ANOVA measures rather than simply executing statistical software.

Looking back across projects, I also noticed that my engagement with AI and machine learning often remains at the application layer — integrating OCR pipelines, LLM APIs, or computer vision — rather than exploring those systems in depth. Becoming aware of that pattern made me more conscious of the difference between using advanced technological tools and understanding them.
